q: Summarize the book of Hebrews: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The human author is unknown; the letter is anonymous, though it has always been received as canonical and inspired. Likely written before A.D. 70, since it speaks of the temple sacrifices as ongoing.

**Genre.** Epistle (a "word of exhortation," sermonic in style).

**Theme.** Christ is supremely better than every Old Covenant institution, so the readers must not drift away or apostatize but persevere in faith. Written to Jewish Christians tempted to retreat to the old order under pressure.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — Christ superior to angels, Moses, and Joshua's rest
- 4:14–7:28 — Christ our great High Priest after the order of Melchizedek
- 8–10 — The better covenant, sanctuary, and once-for-all sacrifice
- 11 — The roll call of faith
- 12–13 — Endurance, discipline, and final exhortations

**Christ & significance.** Hebrews presents Jesus as the final word of God, our great High Priest who offered himself once for all and now intercedes, fulfilling and superseding the entire sacrificial system.
summary: Author unknown (anonymous), likely before A.D. 70. Christ is better than every Old Covenant institution; persevere and do not fall away, for he is our great High Priest.

q: Summarize the book of James: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** James, the Lord's brother and leader of the Jerusalem church; likely an early letter, c. A.D. 45–49, possibly the earliest New Testament epistle.

**Genre.** Epistle (wisdom literature in style, echoing Proverbs and Jesus' Sermon on the Mount).

**Theme.** Genuine, living faith proves itself in works and practical godliness; James confronts a faith that is merely verbal and exhorts the scattered believers to wisdom, patience, and consistency.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Trials, testing, wisdom, and hearing versus doing
- 2 — Impartiality and faith that works
- 3 — Taming the tongue and heavenly wisdom
- 4 — Worldliness, humility, and submission to God
- 5 — Warning to the rich, patience, and prayer

**Christ & significance.** James applies the ethic of Christ's kingdom to daily life, showing that saving faith is never alone but bears the fruit of obedience, complementing Paul's teaching on justification.
summary: James the Lord's brother, c. A.D. 45–49. Real faith works: practical wisdom on trials, the tongue, the poor, and prayer.

q: Summarize the book of 1 Peter: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The apostle Peter; written from Rome ("Babylon") c. A.D. 62–64, shortly before the Neronian persecution.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Living hope and holy conduct in the face of suffering: Peter encourages scattered believers, "elect exiles," to stand firm under unjust persecution, anchored in Christ's resurrection and coming glory.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–2:10 — The living hope and the calling to be God's holy people
- 2:11–3:12 — Submission and witness in society, household, and marriage
- 3:13–4:19 — Suffering for righteousness as Christ suffered
- 5 — Shepherding the flock, humility, and final encouragement

**Christ & significance.** Christ's innocent suffering, atoning death, and resurrection are the pattern and ground of the believer's hope, making present trials the pathway to glory.
summary: The apostle Peter, c. A.D. 62–64. A living hope and holy conduct for elect exiles suffering for their faith, grounded in Christ's example.

q: Summarize the book of 2 Peter: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The apostle Peter; written near the end of his life, c. A.D. 64–68, as a final testament before his death.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Growth in true knowledge of Christ as a defense against false teachers and scoffers who deny the Lord's return; Peter urges believers to remember apostolic truth and to live godly lives in expectation of the day of God.

**Outline.**
- 1 — The call to grow in grace, knowledge, and Christian virtues
- 2 — Exposure and condemnation of false teachers
- 3 — The certainty of Christ's return and the new heavens and earth

**Christ & significance.** Christ's transfiguration confirms the apostolic message, and his promised return guarantees final judgment and the new creation, motivating holy living and steadfastness.
summary: The apostle Peter, c. A.D. 64–68, a farewell letter. Grow in the knowledge of Christ, resist false teachers, and await the Lord's certain return.

q: Summarize the book of 1 John: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The apostle John; written from Ephesus, c. A.D. 85–95.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Assurance of eternal life and tests of genuine faith: written against an early proto-Gnostic denial of Christ's incarnation, John shows that true believers walk in the light, love one another, and confess Jesus as the Christ come in the flesh.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–2:2 — The Word of life and walking in the light
- 2:3–27 — Knowing God shown by obedience and love; warning against the world
- 2:28–4:6 — Children of God, righteousness, love, and testing the spirits
- 4:7–5:21 — God is love, faith that overcomes, and assurance of life

**Christ & significance.** Jesus is the eternal Word made flesh, the propitiation for sin and advocate with the Father, in whom believers may know with confidence that they have eternal life.
summary: The apostle John, c. A.D. 85–95. Tests of genuine faith and assurance of eternal life: walking in the light, love, and confessing Christ come in the flesh.

q: Summarize the book of 2 John: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The apostle John ("the elder"); written from Ephesus, c. A.D. 85–95.

**Genre.** Epistle (a brief personal letter).

**Theme.** Walking in truth and love while guarding against deceivers: John commends a "chosen lady" and her children for their faithfulness and warns them not to welcome traveling teachers who deny that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh.

**Outline.**
- 1–3 — Greeting in truth and love
- 4–6 — Walking in obedience to the command to love
- 7–11 — Warning against deceivers and false teaching
- 12–13 — Closing greetings

**Christ & significance.** Holding fast the apostolic teaching that Christ came in the flesh is the boundary of fellowship, so love and doctrinal fidelity belong together.
summary: The apostle John, c. A.D. 85–95. A short letter urging believers to walk in truth and love while refusing hospitality to deceivers who deny Christ's incarnation.

q: Summarize the book of 3 John: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The apostle John ("the elder"); written from Ephesus, c. A.D. 85–95.

**Genre.** Epistle (a brief personal letter).

**Theme.** Hospitality and support for faithful gospel workers: John commends Gaius for receiving traveling missionaries, rebukes the domineering Diotrephes who refuses them, and praises the good example of Demetrius.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — Greeting and joy over Gaius walking in truth
- 5–8 — Commendation for supporting traveling missionaries
- 9–10 — Rebuke of Diotrephes for his pride and rejection of others
- 11–14 — The example of Demetrius and closing greetings

**Christ & significance.** Faithfully supporting those who labor for "the Name" advances the truth of Christ, while pride and self-promotion in the church work against it.
summary: The apostle John, c. A.D. 85–95. A short letter praising Gaius for hospitality to gospel workers and rebuking the proud Diotrephes who opposed them.

q: Summarize the book of Jude: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Jude, the brother of James and of the Lord; written c. A.D. 65–80.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Contend for the faith against ungodly intruders: Jude urgently calls believers to defend the apostolic gospel and resist false teachers who twist grace into license, warning by examples of past divine judgment.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — Greeting and the call to contend for the faith
- 5–16 — Historical and prophetic warnings against the ungodly
- 17–23 — Exhortation to persevere, build up, and rescue others
- 24–25 — Doxology to the God who keeps his people

**Christ & significance.** Christ keeps and will present his people blameless, and Jude's closing doxology anchors perseverance in God's power to guard those he has called.
summary: Jude the Lord's brother, c. A.D. 65–80. An urgent call to contend for the faith against false teachers, ending in a doxology to the God who keeps his people.

q: Summarize the book of Revelation: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** The apostle John; written c. A.D. 95 during exile on the island of Patmos under the emperor Domitian.

**Genre.** Apocalyptic / Prophecy (in the form of a circular letter to the churches).

**Theme.** The unveiling of Jesus Christ as the sovereign, reigning Lamb who judges evil, vindicates his suffering people, and brings history to its consummation in a new creation. It encourages persecuted believers to faithful endurance, assuring them that Christ wins.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Vision of the glorified Christ
- 2–3 — Letters to the seven churches
- 4–5 — Heavenly throne room and the worthy Lamb
- 6–16 — Visions of seals, trumpets, and bowls of judgment
- 17–20 — Fall of Babylon, the return of Christ, and final judgment
- 21–22 — The new heaven and new earth and the New Jerusalem

**Christ & significance.** Revelation portrays the crucified Lamb as the risen, reigning Lord of history who will return to defeat evil decisively and dwell with his redeemed people forever in the new creation.
summary: The apostle John, c. A.D. 95 on Patmos. An apocalyptic vision of the reigning Lamb who judges evil and makes all things new, calling the churches to faithful endurance.
